g.data-narration: Welcome to Decision Trees module. We will explore how to prove fundamental lower bounds on algorithm complexity using information theory.
Lower bounds tell us the best possible performance. No algorithm can beat this limit, helping us focus on achievable optimizations.
g.text: • Tell us the BEST possible performance achievable
• Prevent wasting effort trying to beat impossible limits
• Example: Ω(n log n) = optimal for comparison-based sorting

g.data-narration: The comparison model assumes algorithms gain information only through pairwise element comparisons like less-than or greater-than tests.
g.text: Algorithms gain information ONLY through pairwise comparisons:
is a[i] < a[j]? → YES / NO (binary outcome)

g.data-narration: A decision tree is a binary tree modeling comparison-based algorithms. Internal nodes are comparisons, leaves represent correct answers.
The key insight: a binary tree with L leaves must have height at least ceil(log2(L)). This is the foundation for all lower bounds.
g.text: For a binary tree with L leaves, minimum possible height:

g.data-narration: For sorting 3 elements, we have 3! = 6 possible orderings. The minimum height is ceil(log2(6)) = 3 comparisons.
g.text: Number of possible orderings (n distinct elements):

g.data-narration: For n elements, there are n! permutations. The decision tree needs at least n! leaves, giving height ≥ log2(n!) = Ω(n log n).
g.text: Must have at least n! leaves to distinguish all inputs

g.data-narration: Using Stirling's approximation, n! ≈ √(2πn)(n/e)^n, we derive log2(n!) = Θ(n log n), confirming the bound.
For searching in a sorted array of n elements, there are n+1 possible outcomes: element at position 1...n or not found.
Binary search decision tree for n=7 has 8 outcomes (positions 1-7 or not found), requiring exactly 3 comparisons.
Checking if n elements are all unique also requires Ω(n log n) comparisons. There are n! orderings of distinct elements.
g.text: Problem: Are all n elements distinct?

g.data-narration: Merging sorted lists of lengths m and n requires Ω(m log(n/m+1)) comparisons. When m=n, it becomes Ω(n log n).
g.text: Input: Two sorted arrays A(m elements) + B(n elements)
Output: Merged sorted array of m+n elements

g.data-narration: When an algorithm achieves the lower bound, it's optimal. MergeSort and HeapSort achieve O(n log n) - the sorting lower bound is tight.
g.text: When algorithm complexity = lower bound → OPTIMAL

g.data-narration: Non-comparison sorts like Counting Sort can achieve O(n) time because they use additional information beyond comparisons.
g.text: Linear time possible with non-comparison sorts:

g.data-narration: Key formulas to remember: height ≥ ceil(log2(leaves)), sorting Ω(n log n), searching Ω(log n). Decision trees enable optimal algorithm design.
